ITER Vacuum Vessel — Lateral Rattle Analysis

Research findings · 5 000-sample Monte Carlo · scipy HiGHS LP · Displacements magnified ×500 in all diagrams

Executive Summary. The ITER VV rests on 9 toroidal gravity supports each providing ±1.5 mm of toroidal compliance. Because assembly tolerance places each VV bracket at an independent position within its travel range, the vessel can exhibit rigid-body lateral motion (rattle) from its as-assembled location. A Linear Programming analysis bounds this motion: the worst-case rattle range is 3.09 mm (LP bound, valid for any assembly state). Monte Carlo simulation over random assembly states gives P95 = 2.28 mm — 95% of assemblies will rattle less than this. Measuring just two adjacent supports before lock-down reduces P95 to ≈ 2.0 mm.

§1 — Physics Model

Support Mechanism

Each of the 9 gravity supports is a four-bar linkage that accommodates large radial displacements of the VV during assembly and thermal cycling. At the outboard end of the four-bar, a pin with its axis aligned in the toroidal direction connects to a bracket on the VV. The bracket can slide ±1.5 mm along this toroidal pin about the nominal (as-assembled) position — this is the compliance that gives rise to the lateral rattle studied here.

Because the four-bar handles radial motion, the only in-plane compliance that matters for lateral rattle is the toroidal sliding at each pin. No radial gap or lateral slip occurs at the pin itself.

Degrees of Freedom

The VV has three planar rigid-body DOFs: Δx, Δy (lateral translations) and Δθ (in-plane rotation). The toroidal sliding at support i produced by a VV displacement q = [Δx, Δy, Δθ] is:

δᵢ = −sin(φᵢ)·Δx + cos(φᵢ)·Δy + R·Δθ   ≡   A[i,:] · q

where φᵢ = π/2 − 2πi/9 is the toroidal angle of support i, and R = 8 m. Matrix A (9×3) maps the 3 DOFs to the 9 toroidal bracket displacements. ATA = diag(4.5, 4.5, 576), rank 3 — all three DOFs are constrained by the support geometry.

Assembly State

Due to manufacturing and placement tolerances, each bracket does not land at the centre of its travel range at assembly. Instead each bracket settles at an unknown offset ui ~ Uniform(−1.5, +1.5) mm, independently for each support.

Key insight. The 9 assembly offsets ui are independent — they need not be consistent with any single rigid-body displacement. The VV is therefore simultaneously urged by competing offsets at different supports. The "rattle" is the maximum additional rigid-body motion possible from this as-assembled configuration before any bracket reaches its travel limit.

§2 — LP Formulation

Linear Programming (LP) finds the maximum of a linear objective subject to linear inequalities — here, maximising VV displacement in a given direction θ subject to each bracket staying within its ±1.5 mm travel range:

maximise   cos θ · Δx + sin θ · Δy         (displacement in direction θ)

subject to   A · δq  ≤  1.5 mm − u          (remaining travel in + direction)
            −A · δq  ≤  1.5 mm + u          (remaining travel in − direction)

The constraints account for the existing assembly offset: if support i has its bracket already at ui = +1.2 mm, only 0.3 mm of positive toroidal travel remains before the stop is reached. Solving the LP over many directions θ traces out the full rattle envelope of reachable positions from this assembly state.

Verification. Alternating brackets (u = [+1.5, −1.5, +1.5, …] mm): LP rattle = 0.000 mm ✓
Nominal brackets (u = 0): LP rattle = 3.09 mm ✓ (2 × 1.547 mm along ±X)

§3 — Why Rattle Exceeds 3 mm (the Travel Range)

With ±1.5 mm travel, a naive estimate gives 3 mm total range. The LP gives 3.09 mm because the optimum involves simultaneous translation and rotation:

  • Δx = +1.547 mm, Δθ = +0.193 mrad (small coupling rotation)
  • At support 1 (φ ≈ 90°, near the top): the rotation-induced toroidal displacement nearly cancels the translation component, so this support is not binding and imposes no limit on +X motion.
  • At the 4 binding supports: each bracket exactly reaches its +1.5 mm stop, but the rotation has "hidden" part of the displacement, allowing the VV centre to translate 1.547 mm > 1.5 mm.

Each assembly configuration has a principal rattle axis — the direction of maximum reachable motion. The LP feasible region is an elongated polytope, not a circle; the GIFs in §5 animate motion along this principal axis.

§4 — VV State Diagrams

All displacements magnified ×500 for visibility. VV radius = 8 m. At ×500, a 1.5 mm travel range appears as 0.75 m; a 1.5 mm translation appears as 0.75 m.
Key: the machine axis cross (+) is fixed; the blue VV ring and radial spokes move with the vessel; red "|" bars are the toroidal travel stops (fixed to support structure); the coloured dot is the VV bracket position on the toroidal pin (blue = slack, orange = near stop, red = at stop).

§5 — Animated Rattle Motion

Each animation shows the VV rocking through its full rattle range along the principal axis — from maximum backward to maximum forward displacement and back. The radial spokes clearly show the coupled rotation.

§6 — Monte Carlo Results

5 000 independent assembly states were sampled (each ui ~ Uniform(−1.5, +1.5) mm, independently for all 9 supports). For each sample the rattle range was computed by solving the LP over 72 directions. The LP worst-case bound of 3.09 mm is rarely approached — most random assemblies give less motion because the 9 independently-sampled bracket positions compete against one another.

Summary Statistics

StatisticRattle range (mm)Notes
LP worst case (any assembly)3.09Upper bound — rarely achieved
MC maximum (n = 5 000)2.90Worst observed random sample
P992.561 in 100 assemblies exceed this
P952.28Recommended design reference
P902.12
P751.84
P50 (median)1.51Typical assembly

Why Isn't the Reduction Larger?

With 9 supports one might expect the rattle to be substantially smaller than the 3.09 mm single-bracket limit. The reason it is not is structural: the LP always has at most 3 simultaneously binding constraints (because the VV has 3 DOFs). The other 6 brackets retain slack and do not limit the motion. Regardless of how many supports are present, the effective constraint at the LP optimum is determined by just 3 of them — a set of 3 non-collinear supports can fully determine the rigid-body state. The additional supports provide mechanical redundancy but do not reduce the rattle envelope proportionally.

The median rattle (1.51 mm) is roughly half the worst case (3.09 mm) because a typical random assembly does not happen to align its bracket positions optimally for maximum motion — competing offsets at different supports partially cancel.

§7 — Effect of Measuring Supports Before Lock-Down

If the assembly team measures the actual bracket position at k supports before final lock-down (e.g. with feeler gauges), those ui values become known. The LP is then solved with exact values for those supports, tightening the remaining uncertainty distribution. The simulation fixes supports 0 … k−1 (adjacent supports) to the worst-case MC sample values; the remaining 9−k are still randomly sampled.

Interpretation

Measuring 2 adjacent supports before lock-down reduces P95 from 2.28 mm to roughly 2.0 mm. The reduction is modest because adjacent supports share similar toroidal directions, providing correlated information. Measuring supports spread evenly around the vessel (3 at 120° spacing) would give better angular coverage and a larger uncertainty reduction. If all 9 supports are measured the problem becomes fully deterministic — one LP solve gives the exact rattle bound for that specific configuration.

§8 — LP Bounds by Degree of Freedom

DOFLP bound (any assembly state)Total range
Δx (lateral)±1.547 mm3.09 mm
Δy (lateral)±1.523 mm3.05 mm
Δθ (in-plane rotation)±0.1875 mrad0.375 mrad

The slight X/Y asymmetry arises from the discrete support spacing: support 1 is exactly at φ = 90° (pure +Y direction), making the Y constraint marginally tighter.

§9 — Recommendations for the Assembly Team

#RecommendationImpact
1Use P95 = 2.28 mm as the design rattle allowanceCovers 95% of possible assemblies
2Measure bracket position at 2–3 supports before lock-downP95 drops to ~2.0 mm; fully deterministic if all 9 measured
3For conservative planning use the LP bound of 3.09 mmValid regardless of assembly state
4Note rattle = 0 is achievable with deliberate shimming (alternating offsets)Option for the tightest tolerance assemblies
